title: Repository Sandbox Serving — Browser Sandbox Cell
context: ADR 014 defined the Tribunus Cell as a sovereign local authority domain. This ADR extends the model to multi-user collaboration through a browser sandbox Cell. The owning Cell must never let other Cells mutate the original project workspace directly. Instead, it publishes a controlled working copy into a browser-executable sandbox, lets collaborators operate inside that copy, and receives back a reviewable change envelope. The owning Cell remains the authority that decides whether those changes become part of the real local project. Note: This ADR is extended by ADR 0018 (SDK and Plugin Architecture) to include plugin sandbox patterns and plugin change proposal handling.
decision:
### WebContainer (StackBlitz) — Architecture and State 2026
WebContainer is a browser-based runtime for Node.js, executing entirely inside a browser tab using WASM, ServiceWorker-based virtualized TCP networking, and a virtual filesystem. Boots in milliseconds. Supports npm/pnpm/yarn natively. Uses cross-origin isolation (COEP: require-corp or credentialless). Exposes mount() accepting FileSystemTree or binary snapshots from @webcontainer/snapshot (uses @msgpack/msgpack for serialization). export() can output json, binary, or zip formats. Supports spawn() for processes with terminal I/O via WritableStream/ReadableStream. Only one WebContainer instance can run concurrently per page. Self-hostable via StackBlitz Enterprise. Works in Chromium, Firefox, and Safari TP. Key limitations: single concurrent instance, requires cross-origin isolation headers, no native non-JS language runtimes (unless ported via WASI).

Confidence: high

### Origin Private File System (OPFS) — Browser Virtual Filesystem
OPFS is a standard WHATWG Living Standard (updated March 2026). Provides a private, origin-scoped virtual filesystem on disk. 3-4x faster than IndexedDB. Supports synchronous read/write exclusively in Web Workers via FileSystemSyncAccessHandle. Accessed via navigator.storage.getDirectory(). Subject to browser storage quota. Cleared when user clears site data. No permission prompts needed for OPFS access. Chromium-based browsers support the full File System Access API (including user-visible file pickers); Firefox and Safari support only OPFS (no local-disk pickers). A 2026 vulnerability (FROST) demonstrated side-channel timing attacks through OPFS.

Confidence: high

### Browser Sandboxing Mechanisms — iframe sandbox, COOP, COEP, CSP
Modern browsers use multi-process sandboxing with a broker process model. The iframe sandbox attribute provides fine-grained permission control via tokens (allow-scripts, allow-same-origin, allow-forms, allow-popups, allow-modals, allow-downloads, allow-top-navigation, allow-storage-access-by-user-activation, etc.). Cross-Origin Isolation requires both COOP: same-origin AND COEP: require-corp (or credentialless). COEP require-corp blocks cross-origin no-cors resources unless they set CORP: cross-origin. COEP credentialless allows embedding without CORP but strips credentials. COEP credentialless can be paired with iframe credentialless for ephemeral contexts. Cross-origin isolation is required for SharedArrayBuffer, unthrottled Performance.now(), and WebContainer. The allow attribute on iframes supports Permissions Policy for fine-grained feature control. CSP via the csp attribute on iframes adds another layer.

Confidence: high

### Collaborative Editing — Monaco, CodeMirror, Yjs Integration
Yjs is the leading CRDT library for browser-based collaborative editing in 2026. Two primary editor stacks: CodeMirror 6 + y-codemirror.next (lighter, more modular, better mobile behavior) and Monaco Editor + y-monaco (VS Code-like experience, heavier). Transport choices: y-websocket (simple client-server), Hocuspocus (production-grade with auth, persistence, multiplexing), Liveblocks (managed infrastructure), y-webrtc (P2P, prototypes). Key patterns: one Y.Text per file for multi-file editors, Y.UndoManager for collaboration-safe undo/redo, awareness for cursors/presence. Yjs separates editor binding from network provider cleanly. CodeMirror 6 recommended as default for most web apps unless VS Code features are required.

Confidence: high

### Browser-Based Terminal Emulation — xterm.js Architecture
xterm.js is the dominant browser terminal emulator, used by VS Code, JupyterLab, Theia, Replit, and many others. Implements full VT220/xterm terminal emulator in JavaScript. Communicates via WebSocket/WebRTC to a backend process. Core package @xterm/xterm with addons: addon-fit, addon-search, addon-web-links, addon-webgl (GPU-accelerated rendering), addon-serialize, addon-image, addon-unicode-graphemes. Browser-native PTY alternatives: WebContainer provides pty-like I/O via WebContainerProcess.input (WritableStream) and .output (ReadableStream). Other approaches: node-pty via WebSocket bridge (VS Code), GoTTY/ttyd (Go-based PTY to WebSocket), v86 (x86 emulator in WASM running full Linux). No true browser-native PTY exists without a backend process or WASM runtime server.

Confidence: high

### Sandbox Security — Capability-Based Access and Permission Models
Capability-based access control is implemented in WASI (WebAssembly System Interface), where Wasm modules receive explicit keys (capabilities) for resources (file handles, network sockets, subprocess). WebAssembly provides memory-safe sandboxed execution. The sandbox attribute on iframes provides a capability-like model: each allow-* token is a capability grant. CSP provides declarative capability restrictions. Permission Policy (allow attribute) restricts feature access. The browser itself is increasingly the new endpoint with 25% enterprise dedicated browser adoption by 2026. Key principle: the sandbox should combine isolation (iframe sandbox + COEP) with explicit capability grants for resources the sandbox cell needs to function.

Confidence: high

### Snapshot and Patch Patterns for Virtual Filesystem Sandboxes
Three primary snapshot/merge patterns: (1) WebContainer native pattern: mount(FileSystemTree | Uint8Array snapshot) -> execute code -> export(format: json | binary | zip) for full filesystem export. Uses @webcontainer/snapshot with msgpack serialization. (2) Container layer pattern: layered filesystem snapshots (like Docker), where each layer is a diff. (3) Git-like diff/patch: compute patches between snapshots using standard diff algorithms, apply patches to merge sandbox changes back to origin. Export the sandbox filesystem, generate diff against original mounted tree, apply diff selectively. Exclude patterns (node_modules, build artifacts) important for efficient diffs.

Confidence: high
consequences:
### Comparison of Browser Execution Sandbox Approaches
Three tiers of browser sandbox execution: (1) WebContainer - full Node.js runtime, most capable, heaviest, requires COEP, single instance only. (2) iframe sandbox with service workers - lighter, multiple instances possible, limited to browser APIs, no Node.js runtime. (3) WebAssembly via WASI - language-agnostic, memory-safe, capability-based, no system calls without explicit grant. For a Sandbox Cell in ADR 0016: the iframe sandbox approach with postMessage communication is the most flexible for general-purpose compute cells. Combine iframe sandbox (allow-scripts, allow-same-origin for controlled access) + CSP + ServiceWorker for network isolation + postMessage for controlled capability delegation. For Node.js execution, WebContainer can be embedded. For multi-language execution, WASI-based Wasm modules in Workers are ideal.

Confidence: medium
